description: Asking for and giving directions, describing locations

items.notes: 'Missä' is the inessive form of 'mikä' (what), literally 'in what/where'. This is the most common way to ask for locations in Finnish.

items.english: How do I get to the center?
items.notes: 'Keskustaan' is the illative case (-an) of 'keskusta', meaning 'into the center'. The illative expresses movement toward or into. 'Päästä' means 'to get to / to reach'.
Informal imperative (sinä). 'Vasemmalle' is the allative case (-lle) of 'vasen', indicating direction toward. Formal: 'Kääntykää vasemmalle.'
Informal imperative (sinä). 'Oikealle' is the allative case (-lle) of 'oikea', indicating direction toward. Formal: 'Kääntykää oikealle.'
Informal imperative of 'mennä'. 'Eteenpäin' means 'forward/onward' — 'eteen' (to the front) + '-päin' (toward). 'Suoraan' is the illative of 'suora' (straight), used adverbially.
'Lähellä' is the adessive case (-llä) of 'lähi' (near), functioning as a postposition meaning 'near/nearby'. Finnish uses locative cases where English uses prepositions.
'Kaukana' is an essive-like adverb from 'kauka-' (far). It indicates static location far away. For movement away: 'kauas' (to far); from far: 'kaukaa'.

'Vasemmalla' is the adessive (-lla) of 'vasen' (left), meaning 'on the left'. 'Puolella' is the adessive of 'puoli' (side), meaning 'on the side of'. Both use the adessive case for location on a surface.
'Oikealla' is the adessive (-lla) of 'oikea' (right), meaning 'on the right'. 'Puolella' is the adessive of 'puoli' (side). The adessive case indicates being on/at a location.

'Kävelymatkan' is the genitive of 'kävelymatka' (walking distance). 'Päässä' is the inessive of 'pää' (end/head), used as a postposition meaning 'at a distance of'. The '-ko' suffix on 'on' makes it a yes/no question.
'Kuinka' means 'how' (for degree/amount). 'Kaukana' (far away) is a locative adverb. Finnish word order is flexible — the question word comes first.

items.english: Where is the nearest bus stop?
items.notes: 'Lähin' is the superlative of 'lähellä' (near), meaning 'nearest'. 'Bussipysäkki' is a compound word: 'bussi' (bus) + 'pysäkki' (stop). Finnish forms compounds by joining words directly.
'Rautatieasema' is a compound: 'rautatie' (railroad, literally 'iron-road') + 'asema' (station). Finnish loves compound nouns. 'Missä' (inessive of 'mikä') asks about location.

'Tätä' is the partitive of 'tämä' (this). 'Katua' is the partitive of 'katu' (street). 'Pitkin' is a postposition meaning 'along' that governs the partitive case. Finnish postpositions follow their noun.

'Seuraavasta' is the elative (-sta) of 'seuraava' (next). 'Risteyksestä' is the elative of 'risteys' (intersection). The elative case here indicates the point where the turning action occurs — 'from/at the intersection'.

items.english: It's on the other side of the street.
items.notes: 'Kadun' is the genitive of 'katu' (street). 'Toisella' is the adessive (-lla) of 'toinen' (other/second). 'Puolella' is the adessive of 'puoli' (side). The genitive + adessive postposition pattern is very common for expressing location.

'Näetkö' is 'nähdä' (to see) conjugated for 'sinä' with the question suffix -kö. 'Tuon' is the genitive/accusative of 'tuo' (that). 'Rakennuksen' is the genitive/accusative of 'rakennus' (building). The object takes accusative/genitive case with a positive verb.
'Aivan' means 'quite/exactly/right'. 'Tuossa' is the inessive (-ssa) of 'tuo' (that), meaning 'in/at that (place)'. Finnish demonstrative pronouns inflect for locative cases to express location.
'Sillan' is the genitive of 'silta' (bridge). 'Yli' is a postposition meaning 'over/across' that governs the genitive case. Note the consonant gradation: 'silta' → 'sillan' (t → nothing in the stem).

items.english: Can you show on the map?
items.notes: 'Voitko' is 'voida' (can) with sinä conjugation + question suffix -ko. 'Näyttää' means 'to show'. 'Kartalla' is the adessive (-lla) of 'kartta' (map), meaning 'on the map'. The adessive indicates location on a surface.

items.english: How do I get back to the hotel?
items.notes: 'Päästä' means 'to get to / to reach'. 'Takaisin' = back. 'Hotellille' is allative case of 'hotelli' (to/onto the hotel).

'Missä' (inessive of 'mikä') asks about location. 'Me' is 'we'. 'Olemme' is the first person plural of 'olla' (to be). The pronoun 'me' is often included in speech for clarity, though technically optional.

'Onko' is 'olla' + question suffix -ko. 'Täällä' means 'here' (adessive). 'Lähellä' means 'nearby' (adessive). 'Pankkia' is the partitive of 'pankki' (bank) — partitive is used in existential sentences to express 'some/a bank'.

items.english: How do I get to the airport?
items.notes: 'Lentokentälle' is the allative (-lle) of 'lentokenttä' (airport, literally 'flying-field'), meaning 'to/onto the airport'. The allative indicates movement toward. Compare with illative 'keskustaan' (item 2) — Finnish has two 'to' cases depending on the noun.

'Ensimmäinen' means 'first' (ordinal). 'Katu' is 'street' in nominative. 'Oikealla' is the adessive (-lla) of 'oikea' (right), meaning 'on the right'. This is a short directive phrase — no verb needed.
'Toinen' means 'second' (also 'other/another'). 'Vasemmalla' is the adessive (-lla) of 'vasen' (left), meaning 'on the left'. Same verbless directive pattern as item 25.

items.finnish: Se on noin viisi minuuttia kävellen.
items.english: It's about five minutes on foot.
items.literalTranslation: It is about five minute-partitive walking.
items.notes: 'Noin' means 'about/approximately'. 'Minuuttia' is the partitive of 'minuutti' (minute) — partitive is required after numerals greater than 1. 'Kävellen' is the instructive form of 'kävellä' (to walk), meaning 'by walking/on foot'.
